train_pairs.target: I gave my canoe to her.
I gave my canoe to her.
The man brought my blood to the woman.
She brought your_{sg} banana to the man.
The man gave the woman’s bone to me.
He brought the man’s eye to me.

test_pairs.target: You_{sg} gave the man’s house to him.

steps.accumulated_dict: nuta - I, sykaru - my canoe, nykanawate - gave, uwamukary - to her, pita - You_{sg}, ãtary - drank, ĩpurãã - the water, kyky - the man, mynaru - brought, nyherẽka - my blood, sytumukary - to the woman, herẽkatxi - the woman, uãtary - drank, sytu - the blood
steps.accumulated_rules:
1. The language may have a flexible word order, as "nuta sykaru nykanawate uwamukary" and "nykanawate nysykaru uwamukary" both translate to "I gave my canoe to her", suggesting that the position of the verb and the subject can be swapped.
2. The language appears to use suffixes or prefixes to indicate possession, as "sykaru" (my canoe) and "nyherẽka" (my blood) have a prefix "ny-" or "sy-" which might indicate the first person singular possessive.
3. The verb "nykanawate" (gave) and "uãtary" (drank) seem to be in the past tense, but the exact mechanism for forming the past tense is unclear.

hypotheses: nuta sykaru nykanawate uwamukary → I gave my canoe to her.
pita ãtary ĩpurãã → You drank the water.
kyky mynaru nyherẽka sytumukary → The man brought my blood to the woman.
references: You_{sg} gave the man’s house to him.
